timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Ark Brought to Jerusalem

heb.paragraph: "The earth is the LORD’s (la-YHWH), and everything in it" (v.1). Total divine ownership. Entrance requirements (vv.3–4): clean hands, pure heart, no idolatry, no false oaths. Psalm 15’s companion entrance liturgy.

refs.note: "The earth is the Lord’s, and everything in it" — Paul quotes v.1.

notes.note: "The earth is the LORD’s." Ownership precedes worship. You enter HIS house.
"Clean hands and a pure heart" — the entrance requirements. Outer conduct (hands) and inner character (heart).

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the opening declaration is cosmological — the earth belongs to Yahweh not by conquest but by creation. The verb yāsad ('founded') implies architectural design: God laid foundations.
Alter: the entrance liturgy (vv.3-6) shifts from cosmic scope to moral particulars. 'Clean hands' is outward conduct; 'pure heart' is inward intention — the psalm demands both.
Calvin: if the earth is the Lord's, then no creature may claim ownership independent of him. All human dominion is stewardship, not sovereignty. Calvin grounds property theology here.
Calvin: 'Who may ascend?' — the question itself is humbling. The mountain of the Lord is not approached casually. Calvin reads this as the prerequisite of genuine worship: moral seriousness.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: 'the world' (tēvēl) refers to the inhabited world, the productive land — distinct from 'ereṣ (the earth as a whole). The parallel is not redundant but narrows from planet to population.
NET: 'who does not trust in an idol' — the Hebrew reads 'who has not lifted up his soul to what is false' (lāšāw'). Some MSS read 'my soul' (nafshî), but the 3rd person is better attested.
Kidner: the psalm begins with the widest possible lens — everything, everywhere — before narrowing to one hill, one gate, one question. The rhetorical funnel is deliberate.

Kidner: four conditions for four qualities: clean hands (action), pure heart (motive), not lifting the soul to what is false (worship), not swearing deceitfully (speech). The whole person is examined.
VanGemeren: the creation theology here is not abstract cosmology but covenant foundation. Because Yahweh made the world, he has the right to set conditions for approaching him.
VanGemeren: 'vindication from God their Savior' — ṣedāqâ here is not earned righteousness but a gift received. The worshipper ascends not on merit but by grace-enabled obedience.
Goldingay: the psalm's opening is a creation confession that functions as a counter-claim against Canaanite religion. If Yahweh founded the world on the seas, Ba'al's storm claims are void.
Goldingay: 'the generation of those who seek him' — dôr is not merely 'generation' (temporal) but 'circle' or 'company' (social). It describes a community defined by its orientation toward God.

hist.context: The creation claim (v.1) grounds the entrance requirements (vv.3–6). Because God owns everything, entering his presence requires holiness.

sections.header: Verses 7–10 — “Lift Up Your Heads, You Gates!”

heb.paragraph: "Who is this King of Glory (melek̅ hakkābōd)? The LORD strong and mighty, the LORD mighty in battle!" (v.8). The gates of Jerusalem (or heaven) are summoned to lift their lintels for the arriving King. Traditionally associated with the ark entering Jerusalem (2 Sam 6).

refs.note: David brings the ark to Jerusalem — the possible setting.
Christ seated "far above all rule and authority" — the ascension fulfilment.

notes.note: "Lift up your heads, you gates!" The gates are personified — they have heads to lift. The architecture responds to the King’s approach.
"The LORD Almighty — he is the King of Glory." The final identification. The question "who is this?" receives its definitive answer.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: 'Lift up your heads, O gates' — the personification is striking. The gates are addressed as living beings who must raise themselves to admit someone greater than their architecture can contain.
Alter: the antiphonal exchange ('Who is this King of glory?') is liturgical drama. The question is not ignorance — it is a cue for proclamation. The answer thunders: 'Yahweh strong and mighty.'

Calvin: the gates lifting their heads signifies that no earthly structure — not the temple, not the city — is grand enough for God. He must stoop to enter what we build. Calvin reads this christologically.
Calvin: 'Yahweh of Hosts' — the final title gathers all the divine attributes: creator (v.1), moral lawgiver (v.4), warrior (v.8), and cosmic sovereign. The psalm ends at its highest pitch.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: 'ancient doors' (pitḥê 'ôlām) — 'ôlām can mean 'ancient' or 'everlasting.' The gates are either age-old (emphasising their venerability) or eternal (emphasising divine permanence).
NET: 'Lord Almighty' translates Yahweh ṣebā'ôt ('Yahweh of armies/hosts'). The title evokes both heavenly armies (angelic hosts) and Israel's military forces under divine command.
Kidner: the processional dialogue is designed for two choirs — one outside demanding entry, one inside demanding credentials. The effect is cumulative: each repetition raises the intensity.
Kidner: the psalm moves from creation (vv.1-2) to character (vv.3-6) to coronation (vv.7-10). The logic is clear: the God who made the world and defines holiness now enters in triumph.
VanGemeren: the entrance liturgy anticipates messianic fulfillment. The King of Glory entering the gates prefigures Christ's triumphal entry and ultimately his ascension to the heavenly throne.
VanGemeren: 'mighty in battle' — Yahweh as warrior is not metaphor but theological reality. The God who defeated chaos at creation (v.2) continues to fight for his people in history.
Goldingay: the command to the gates recalls the ark procession — the physical symbol of Yahweh's presence entering Jerusalem. The psalm likely accompanied an actual liturgical enactment.
Goldingay: the final answer identifies the King of Glory as 'Yahweh of Hosts' — the most militarily charged divine title. The psalm ends not in contemplation but in martial acclamation.

hist.context: The processional liturgy: gates summoned to open for the King of Glory. "Who is he?" asked twice, answered twice: the LORD strong and mighty, the LORD Almighty.

ppl.name: God (Yahweh / King of Glory)
ppl.text: Yahweh's ownership claim ('the earth is the LORD's,' v.1) rests on his creative act: he 'founded it on the seas' (v.2), echoing the ancient Near Eastern motif of divine victory over chaotic waters. The climactic title 'King of Glory' (melek ha-kavod, vv.7-10) appears only here in the Psalter. The antiphonal gate-lifting dialogue (vv.7-10) likely accompanied the ark's procession into Jerusalem (2 Sam 6) or the temple.

ppl.name: The Righteous Worshiper
ppl.role: One who may ascend God's holy hill (vv.3-6)
ppl.text: Entry requirements include 'clean hands and a pure heart' (v.4) — external conduct and internal integrity. The prohibition against lifting one's soul to 'an idol' (shav, also 'emptiness/falsehood') connects worship purity to the third commandment. This entrance liturgy genre (cf. Ps 15, Isa 33:14-16) reflects actual temple-gate questioning of pilgrims about their moral fitness.

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: Attributed author; the king who brought the ark to Zion
ppl.text: If composed for the ark's entry into Jerusalem (2 Sam 6:12-15), David authored a psalm that merges creation theology, ethical instruction, and processional worship into a single liturgy. The three-part structure (cosmic claim → moral demand → triumphal entry) became a template for later enthronement psalms.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe one who has clean hands and a pure heart, who does not trust in an idol or swear by a false god.ESVHe who has clean hands and a pure heart, who does not lift up his soul to what is false and does not swear deceitfully.NIVThe earth is the Lord’s, and everything in it, the world, and all who live in it;ESVThe earth is the LORD\u2019s and the fullness thereof, the world and those who dwell therein,

rec.text: ""The earth is the LORD’s." Ownership precedes worship. You enter HIS house."

rows.label: The Claim — 24:1–6
rows.text: "The earth is the LORD’s." Clean hands, pure heart. Who may ascend?

hebtext.note: "The earth is the LORD’s (la-YHWH), and everything in it" (v.1). Total divine ownership. Entrance requirements (vv.3–4): clean hands, pure heart, no idolatry, no false oaths. Psalm 15’s companion entrance liturgy.

"Who is this King of Glory (melek̅ hakkābōd)? The LORD strong and mighty, the LORD mighty in battle!" (v.8). The gates of Jerusalem (or heaven) are summoned to lift their lintels for the arriving King. Traditionally associated with the ark entering Jerusalem (2 Sam 6).

thread.text: "The earth is the Lord’s, and everything in it" — Paul quotes v.1.
David brings the ark to Jerusalem — the possible setting.
Christ seated "far above all rule and authority" — the ascension fulfilment.

themes.note: Sovereignty and Worship peak: the King of Glory enters. Holiness: entrance requires purity. Prophecy: the ascension tradition.
